1: The attribution formula marks Elihu’s third speech (Job 35), which is the shortest of his four (only 16 verses) and focuses on a single theological argument: the relationship between human righteousness/wickedness and the transcendent God. The brevity relative to Job 33–34 suggests a focused rebuttal of a specific claim rather than a broad survey; Elihu zeroes in on the transactional-religion critique and the problem of unanswered prayer.
2: “Do you think this is just when you say, ‘My righteousness is more than God’s’?” presents what Elihu identifies as the logical implication of Job’s position. Job has not literally said this, but Elihu claims it follows from Job’s assertion that he is righteous and God has wronged him: if Job is righteous and God’s treatment of him is unjust, then by the comparative logic of the retribution system, Job’s righteousness exceeds God’s. In ANE wisdom thought, the claim of superior righteousness to a deity was the most extreme form of hubris — comparable to the hybris (ὕβρις) concept in Greek tragedy.
3: “For you say, ‘What does it benefit you? What do I gain from not sinning?’” quotes the transactional-religion calculus that Job has used in Job 21:14–15 and Job 22:2–3 (in Eliphaz’s formulation). The question “what do I gain?” (mah–yiskon lāk, what does it profit you?) applies instrumental reasoning to religious devotion. In ANE religious economy, offerings and piety were understood as investments that generated divine favor; Elihu identifies the logical endpoint of this transactional model: if the investment yields no return, it is not made. The adversary’s hypothesis (Job 1:9: “does Job fear God for nothing?”) is here attributed to Job himself.
4: “I will give you an answer, and your companions along with you” extends the response to the three friends who have shared similar transactional assumptions about divine reward. The inclusion of “your companions” suggests that Elihu sees the transactional-religion error as endemic to the entire debate — Job has raised it explicitly, but the three friends’ retributionism implicitly shared the same framework. By addressing all four together, Elihu claims to correct a foundational error underlying the entire dialogue.
5: “Look up at the sky and see; behold the clouds, higher than you” uses spatial transcendence as an argument for divine transcendence. In ANE cosmology, the heavens (šāmayyim) were the highest spatial domain — the divine realm separated from the human by an unbridgeable vertical distance. The instruction to look up and see the clouds above reinforces the physical intuition of vertical transcendence. The argument from spatial transcendence to metaphysical transcendence (what is higher than you cannot be affected by you) is a form of the cosmological argument for divine aseity that runs throughout the wisdom literature (Isaiah 40:22: “he who sits above the circle of the earth”).
6: “If you sin, what do you accomplish against him? If your transgressions multiply, what do you do to him?” is the first statement of the divine aseity (a se, self-sufficient being) in Job. The point is that human sin cannot harm God’s being or affect his nature: he is metaphysically beyond the reach of human wickedness. This contrasts with the transactional model where sin “injured” the deity (Mesopotamian theology held that the gods could be offended, weakened, or displeased by human neglect). Elihu’s position is more abstract and philosophically sophisticated: God’s perfection is not dependent on human obedience.
7: “If you are righteous, what do you give him? What does he receive from your hand?” extends the aseity argument to righteousness: just as sin cannot harm God, righteousness cannot benefit him. This is the symmetrical positive of v6. In ANE religion, offerings and piety were understood as literally feeding and sustaining the gods (the Mesopotamian temple economy involved feeding statues of the gods elaborate meals). Elihu rejects this model: God is self-sufficient and receives nothing from human hands that he needs. The theological implication is that human righteousness is not a transaction with God but must be oriented differently — toward other humans (v8) or toward God for non-transactional reasons.
8: “Your wickedness harms only someone like yourself, and your righteousness benefits only other people” redirects the effect of human moral action from the divine to the human sphere. This is a significant theological claim: moral action has its primary effect horizontally (between humans), not vertically (toward God). The social ethic of the OT prophets — “what does YHWH require but to do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly” (Micah 6:8) — similarly redirects religious obligation toward human neighbors. Elihu’s point does not deny that righteousness pleases God, but insists that the impact of human moral action is primarily social rather than divine.
9: “Under the weight of great oppression people cry out; they call for help from the arm of the powerful” introduces the social reality that provides the context for the unanswered-prayer argument. The setting is the classic ANE situation of the oppressed crying out for deliverance — the foundational narrative of Exodus 2:23–25 (“the Israelites groaned in their slavery and cried out, and their cry for help went up to God”). But Elihu notes a crucial difference: the cry in v9 is directed “from the arm of the powerful” (mizzᵉrôaʿ rabbîm) — toward human powerful protectors — rather than toward God. The horizontal appeal to human power is the setting for vv10–13.
10: “But no one asks, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?’” identifies the missing element in the oppressed people’s cry: they appeal to human power without seeking the divine. “Where is God my Maker?” (’ayyēh ’ĕlôah ʿōśay) is a lament question that acknowledges divine absence while appealing to divine presence — the form of Psalms 42:3 (“where is your God?”). The remarkable phrase “who gives songs in the night” (nōtēn zᵉmirôt baLlāylāh) appears only here; the night-song in darkness is an image of divine comfort and presence precisely in the most opaque suffering. The ANE night as a time of divine communication (dream-visions) becomes here a time of divine music-giving.
11: “Who teaches us more than the animals of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky” grounds human distinctiveness in the capacity to know God. In ANE wisdom literature, comparisons with animal behavior were a standard instructional device (Proverbs 6:6: “go to the ant, O sluggard”; Job 30:24–31’s animal wisdom sayings). The argument is that humans have a capacity the animals lack: the ability to ask “where is God my Maker?” This distinctively human capacity for God-seeking is the basis for the critique in v10: the oppressed who fail to seek God are not using the uniquely human faculty they have been given.
12: “There they cry, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil people” identifies the reason for unanswered prayer: the moral character of the petitioner. In the Mesopotamian penitential tradition, unanswered prayer was consistently explained by the petitioner’s sin or impurity; the šuilla prayers begin with elaborate self-examination precisely because the petitioner recognizes that their own state may be blocking divine response. The specific cause Elihu names — “the pride of evil people” (gāʾon rᵉšāʿîm) — echoes the anti-pride theme of Job 33:17. The hollow cry that God does not answer is characterized by human pride rather than genuine humility and repentance.
13: “Certainly God does not hear a hollow cry; the Almighty does not regard it” states the principle of genuine vs. performative prayer that has deep roots in the OT and ANE. Isaiah’s critique of Israel’s worship (Isaiah 1:11–15: “I cannot endure evil assemblies; your hands are full of blood”) and Amos’s condemnation (Amos 5:21–23: “I hate, I despise your religious festivals”) both make the same point: ritual performance without moral integrity is not heard. The specific term šāwʾ (hollow, vanity, falsehood) applied to the cry indicates that it is not merely ineffective but fraudulent — speech that claims more than it possesses.
14: “How much less when you say you cannot see him, that the case is before him, and you must wait for him” directly applies the unanswered-prayer principle to Job’s situation. Job has said that he cannot find God (Job 23:3–9) and yet insists his case is before him (Job 23:4: “I would lay out my case before him”). Elihu points out the logical inconsistency: Job simultaneously says he cannot perceive God and insists he has a case pending before God. The instruction to “wait for him” (ḥîl lô, wait in birth-pain expectation) uses the most intense form of waiting — not passive waiting but the active, urgent expectation of one who knows something is coming. Elihu prescribes this waiting as the correct posture.
15: “But now, because God has not punished in his anger and does not take sharp notice of every transgression” identifies divine patience as the theological context for Job’s continued life. Elihu argues that God’s non-immediate punishment is evidence of divine patience rather than divine absence or injustice. The concept of divine long-suffering before judgment is a persistent OT theme (Exodus 34:6: “YHWH, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger”; Nahum 1:3: “YHWH is slow to anger but great in power”). In ANE treaty theology, the delayed enforcement of curses was sometimes interpreted as divine patience giving the transgressor time to repent.
16: “So Job opens his mouth with empty words; he heaps up words without knowledge” is Elihu’s final verdict on Job’s speeches in this third address. The phrase “empty words” (hebel, vanity/breath) is the signature term of Ecclesiastes for the transience and futility of human endeavor; the accusation that Job’s words are hebel places them in the category of the ultimately meaningless. “Without knowledge” (bᵉlî-daʿat) is the same phrase YHWH will use in Job 38:2, creating one of the book’s deliberate echoes: Elihu’s verdict correctly identifies the formal problem (words without knowledge) without understanding the actual situation. It is YHWH’s words from the whirlwind, not Elihu’s, that will give Job what he truly needs.
